trajectory_summary: A clean, efficient call on a warm, cooperative customer that ends with a booked appointment and a bill already in hand — but with two structural vulnerabilities baked in. The wife is excluded from an appointment where her title status is unconfirmed, and Holland made two ungrounded financial promises (savings guarantee, state incentives) that create closer-stage expectation risk. The call's energy was consistently warm but never built; Holland moved through the beats without deepening the emotional anchor Jim offered.
outcome_dispute: The appointment was booked and the bill was captured — a functional success by setter metrics. However, the outcome quality is lower than the raw booking suggests: the wife's exclusion makes this a conditional booking with real no-show or deal-loss risk, and the ungrounded promises create a handoff liability. The v1 tag of 'unknown' should be resolved as 'booked — conditional' rather than a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I mean, so if there's two homeowners, we like them both to be there. But I guess if your wife isn't on the title, then we could just do it with you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland gave Jim permission to exclude his wife at the exact moment the schema says to include her. The title-status was uncertain — Jim said 'maybe my wife' at 01:27 — and Holland resolved that uncertainty in the wrong direction. This appointment is now structurally vulnerable: if the wife is on the title and not on the call, the closer cannot close. The fix is a single reframe: 'Let's plan to have her on the call — the specialist will sort out the title question with both of you.' Manager should walk Holland through the schema's spouse protocol and the SESSION18_fd078269 positive example before Holland's next shift.

key_moments.quote: Well, we we had someone come and visit us, you know, just kinda going door to door talking about solar and said, oh, man. Maybe this is something to look into. Yeah. We've been we've looked a few years back about the with Ionic about buying, and that just wasn't the right fit for us. But this just looking at the different options of the different lease the PPAs, I think they're called, like, leases.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jim handed Holland a rich emotional anchor: a prior solar evaluation that didn't fit, active curiosity about PPAs vs purchase, and a door-knocker as the trigger. The schema marks interest_in_solar as the emotional anchor of the entire pitch, with depth_grading: true and a note that top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Holland's response was 'Mhmm. Yeah.' followed immediately by the credit qualifier. This is a reactive drop, not a strategic deferral — probing Jim's prior Ionic experience and his PPA curiosity is squarely setter-tier work, not closer-tier content.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I mean, with this company, we've we've looked and pulled up the you know, looked at our bills. I mean, it's up to $2.70 in the summer and down to, you know, $1.01 $1.50 in the winter.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland captured the bill range and made a partial flip attempt: 'I know that ComEd is they've been raising their rates quite a bit from what I've heard, so I'm glad you guys are looking into an alternative.' This is a partial activation — it names ComEd as adversary, which is the right instinct. But it landed as a passing comment ('from what I've heard') rather than a pattern interrupt that turns Jim's own number against the utility. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; Holland executed 'ask + acknowledge + soft comment.' The gap is real but not a pure drop.

key_moments.quote: We have a pretty good program right now, I'd say. So what it does is it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland made an ungrounded financial promise: a savings guarantee within the first year. The schema's numeric_discipline is explicit — reps may not invent, estimate, or imply savings not grounded in the call or schema. No bill analysis has been completed at this point in the call; the bill hasn't even been collected yet. This is a compliance gap that creates a closer-stage expectation the closer must either honor or walk back.

key_moments.quote: Yeah, if your credit is above 670, you'll also qualify for any state incentives, as well. So I'll make sure you get those.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland made a blanket incentive guarantee — 'you'll also qualify for any state incentives' and 'I'll make sure you get those.' The schema explicitly notes the tax credit can no longer be guaranteed and that purchase options without the tax credit exist. A blanket state-incentives promise is not authorized by the schema and creates a specific expectation the closer must manage. This compounds the savings-guarantee promise made 8 seconds earlier.
